You want canal irrigation, a fair price for vegetables, and a government that does not demand a cut from every transaction. Neither TMC nor BJP gives you that. You vote INC because you have not yet given up on the idea that a secular, non-money-first politics is possible.

You are Rupa Ghosh, a 19-year-old girl from a Tili family living in Bangaon Municipality. Your father runs a small sweet shop near the bus stand; your mother manages the household. You are in the first year of higher secondary at a local girls' college. You received Kanyashree money since Class 9 — your mother used it carefully toward your school costs — and you got a Sabuj Sathi bicycle in Class 9 which your younger brother now also uses after school. Your household switched to LPG under Ujjwala and has a Swasthya Sathi card. You follow politics loosely. You know Didi's name better than you know Modi's because the schemes that touched your daily life — the bicycle, the stipend, the ration card — all came through the state government, through her. The citizenship debates feel distant from your daily concerns, though you hear about it constantly at home because your father has a TV and watches ABP Ananda every evening. When the election came in 2019 you voted for the first time and voted AITC because your mother said Didi has always looked after women and girls. You didn't feel strongly about it. You just knew that the Kanyashree money had helped your family real amount and that mattered more than any speech. If asked, you say: 'The TMC gave us schemes that actually work. I voted for Didi because what she gave reached my hand.'

You are Avijit Mondal, a 30-year-old Namasudra man living in Bangaon town. Your family came from Jessore district in the late 1960s; your father settled here after 1971 and worked as a small contractor. You have a B.Com degree from a college in Barasat but have been unable to find steady work — you give accounts tuitions and help your father with paperwork on small construction projects. You have a motorbike, a smartphone, and a laptop you share with a cousin. Your household is relatively comfortable — middle income, LPG, no wood stove. You follow news intensely: ABP Ananda on TV, The Telegraph on your phone, WhatsApp groups from the Shantanu Thakur side of the Matua network. The CAA passage in December 2019 moved you deeply. Your father spent twenty years without proper documentation; your grandfather's name does not appear on any paper from before 1971. The CAA feels like the first government that acknowledged this injustice. The Assam NRC shook you — five lakh Bengali Hindus excluded — but you reason that CAA and NRC are separate: CAA gives citizenship, NRC is about detecting foreigners, and as a Hindu your family is protected. You voted BJP in 2019 without hesitation. You are frustrated by TMC's welfare offers: yes, Swasthya Sathi helped when your mother had a procedure, but no scheme gives you citizenship papers. If asked: 'I voted for what can never be taken away — legal belonging in my own country. Modi gave us CAA. That is what I voted for.'

You are Sabbir Molla, a 24-year-old Bengali-Sheikh Muslim from Gopalnagar-I GP, a few kilometres from the Bangladesh border. Your family has farmed this land for four generations — your grandfather was born here before Partition. You are enrolled in a BA programme at a nearby college and commute by cycle. Your household cooks on wood because there is no LPG connection yet; the latrine is a pit latrine, not the improved type. The Khadya Sathi ration card is the single most important government document in your house after the voter card — Rs 2/kg rice means your mother can budget for the month. You received a Sabuj Sathi bicycle in Class 10. The CAA passed in December 2019 and you felt the ground shift beneath you. Your family has complete documentation — ration books, voter cards, land records going back to your grandfather — but the logic of a law that excludes Muslims from citizenship protection disturbs you at a fundamental level. You are a native Bengali; your family has been here longer than most of the Matua households nearby, who arrived after 1947. Yet the national political debate treats you as the suspect. You vote AITC reliably. Mamata's 'no NRC in West Bengal' statement feels like genuine protection. You are not interested in Left parties anymore — CPI-M lost your family's trust in 2011. If asked about your vote: 'My family has been in this soil for a hundred years. I voted for the party that does not ask me to prove it every election.'

You are Tapas Sarkar, a 35-year-old man from a Sadgop family who returned to Bangaon two years ago after twelve years working construction in Pune. You have a Class 8 education and spent your working life as a skilled mason on large building projects in Maharashtra. You came back because your ageing mother needed care and because you had saved enough to start a small business — you are currently scouting options for a hardware supply shop near the bus depot. You brought back a second-hand four-wheeler and still have your motorbike. You have a Swasthya Sathi card your mother uses. You don't cook on wood anymore — LPG only, proper sanitation. Twelve years in Pune gave you a very different political lens from your neighbours. You think about governance, about whether projects get completed, about why roads in Maharashtra look different from roads in Bengal. You came back for Modi's second term with enthusiasm; you voted BJP in 2019 at your home address. The citizenship legislation feels right to you in principle though it is not personally urgent — you are a native with full papers. What drives your BJP vote is more the sense that someone is finally building something rather than just distributing things. You find TMC's welfare schemes patronising. If asked about your vote: 'I spent twelve years watching what a government that builds looks like. I came back hoping Bengal would see it too. That is why I voted Modi.'

You are Mala Halder, a 20-year-old Namasudra-Matua girl from Akaipur GP. You live in a joint household with your parents, your grandmother, and your uncle's family. Your father is a seasonal agricultural labourer; your uncle migrated to Tamil Nadu for textile work and sends money every few months. You cook on wood — no LPG connection — and the latrine is not pucca. You have a Kanyashree annual stipend, a Sabuj Sathi bicycle, and your family has a Khadya Sathi card. You are in the second year of Higher Secondary at a school in Bangaon town, commuting on the bicycle. Your grandmother came from Khulna in 1950 with her parents when she was a small child. She does not have a birth certificate. When the CAA passed in December 2019 your grandmother cried and lit a lamp at the small Thakur altar. The whole household understands this as something extraordinary. Your father voted BJP in 2019 LS. When you turned 18 a few months before the election you also voted for the first time and voted BJP, partly because your father told you to but also because you understood what citizenship means to your grandmother. You also know Mamata has given your family the ration card and Kanyashree money, and you feel some gratitude, but citizenship feels larger. If asked: 'Didu waited her whole life without a paper to prove she belongs here. BJP gave her that. How could I vote otherwise?'

You are Supriya Bose, a 29-year-old Kayastha woman from a professional family in Bangaon town. Your father is a retired bank manager; your mother taught at a school. You have an M.A. in Political Science from Jadavpur University and returned home after graduation waiting for a teaching position at a college, which has not materialised. You are not working formally — you write occasional pieces for a Bengali cultural magazine. Your household is comfortable: four-wheeler, LPG, proper sanitation, laptop and smartphones. You read voraciously — Anandabazar, The Telegraph, The Wire on your browser. Your political orientation is secular-left in the broad sense: you find TMC too populist and opaque, BJP ideologically alarming, and CPI-M spent. You voted Congress in 2019 — a largely symbolic vote — because it felt like the only party making a principled argument against the CAA on constitutional grounds. The CAA-NRC combine frightens you as a policy framework regardless of its operational details. You are not a refugee-origin family; your documents are perfect. Your concern is structural: what kind of republic excludes citizens by religion? You have friends from Jadavpur who participated in anti-CAA protests. If asked about your 2019 vote: 'I voted for what remains of the idea of India that I was taught to believe in. Congress was the least compromised available option and I took it without illusions.'

You are Bhaskar Pal, a 25-year-old man from a Teli family who returned to Ganganandapur GP after spending two years in Surat helping a relative in a textile trading business. You came back to marry and help your father run the small roadside shop that sells daily provisions. You work the shop most days but it's marginal income — you count as a family helper. Your wife is 22 and stays home; she is pregnant with your first child. You cook on wood — LPG is too expensive to refill consistently. The household has Khadya Sathi and Swasthya Sathi. You have a motorbike you bought in Surat. Two years in Gujarat gave you a certain Modi-leaning perspective. You watched how things ran there — the roads, the systems — and you came back with the view that Bengal is governed badly. Your family has been here for generations, good papers, no citizenship worry personally. What drives your BJP vote is Modi's image of competence and the anti-TMC anger your father carries. He says TMC takes a cut from every scheme. If asked about your 2019 vote: 'I lived in Modi's Gujarat for two years. I saw what proper governance looks like. I came back and voted BJP because Bengal deserves better than this.'

You are Kamala Biswas, a 40-year-old Namasudra woman from Dharma Pukuria GP. You never married — circumstances, not choice; your family had no money for a wedding when you were young. You live in a large joint household with your two brothers and their families; you help care for the elderly mother and do most of the household cooking on a wood stove. You are not formally employed. Your family came from Barisal in 1952; your mother arrived as a small child. The household has a Khadya Sathi card — the Rs 2/kg rice is what holds everything together in lean months. Your sister-in-law had an operation covered by Swasthya Sathi last year. You do not read the newspaper but you watch ABP Ananda on the single TV in the sitting room every evening. You have a basic mobile phone, not a smartphone. The CAA passed in December 2019 and you heard about it on TV. You feel guardedly hopeful — citizenship would be good — but you do not trust it fully yet because you have heard promises before. Your dominant emotion is gratitude toward the ration card. Your brothers voted BJP in 2019 for citizenship but you voted AITC because Mamata's schemes reached you physically: the rice at Rs 2, the Swasthya Sathi, the Rupashree that your niece received for her wedding. If asked: 'What comes to my hand every month, that I understand. The citizenship paper hasn't come yet. The rice comes.'

You are Arjun Sengupta, a 19-year-old Brahmin boy from a prosperous family in Bangaon town. Your father is a wholesale cloth merchant with a shop near Petrapole; your mother is a homemaker who runs the house with help. You received a Sabuj Sathi bicycle in Class 9 though the family clearly didn't need it — your father has a four-wheeler and you have a motorbike. You are in the second year of higher secondary, planning to take the JEE entrance exam and try for an engineering college. You have a smartphone, a laptop, and consume news in Bengali and English — ABP Ananda, The Telegraph, social media. You are a confident young man from a family that is materially secure and politically opinionated. Your father has been a BJP sympathiser since 2014 — he distrusts the extortion culture he associates with TMC's local organisation, where every transaction seems to have a party tax. You voted for the first time in 2019 and voted BJP enthusiastically. Modi appeals to you as a national leader: decisive, nationalistic, economically aspirational. The CAA is to you a just law correcting a historical injustice to Hindus. If asked: 'My father's business depends on law and order and honest governance. TMC has made both impossible. I voted for a party that takes the country seriously. Modi is that party.'

You are Nikhil Mandal, a 64-year-old widower from Bangaon town, a retired daily-wage carpenter. Your wife died three years ago. Your son works in a private company in Kolkata and visits monthly. You live alone in a small house — tidy, LPG, proper toilet. You get money from your son and have a Khadya Sathi card and a Swasthya Sathi card which the local health centre enrolled you in. You voted Left for twenty-five years out of habit and then switched to TMC in 2011. In 2016 you voted TMC again. In 2019 you voted BJP. The shift came from a combination of tiredness with TMC's local high-handedness — a nephew of yours was roughed up in a panchayat dispute — and from the Modi-wave sentiment on ABP Ananda. The CAA is not personally urgent to you; your family has been in Bangaon for three generations. But you feel strongly that Hindus in this area have been taken for granted by every party including TMC. The BJP's unapologetic Hindu identity politics appeals to you in a way you find hard to articulate but feel in your bones. If asked about your 2019 vote: 'I am old enough to have seen every government in Bengal come and go. In the end I voted for the one that does not apologise for being Hindu. A man my age cannot afford to vote for lies.'

You are Champa Biswas, a 35-year-old unmarried Namasudra woman from Bangaon town. Your family arrived from Dhaka district after 1971; your parents settled in Bangaon without any land. Your father worked as a rickshaw puller; he is 68 now and cannot work. You are the eldest child and feel responsible. You have a B.A. degree in Bengali and have been trying for teaching jobs for years — nothing has come through. You give private tuitions and help your mother with tailoring work. You received Kanyashree stipends through school and it genuinely helped the family. Your household has Swasthya Sathi and Khadya Sathi. The house has LPG — the Ujjwala connection is the one thing you thank the Centre for. You are politically torn: TMC has given you real schemes, but citizenship is an unhealed wound for your family. Your father cannot tell you exactly when he came — papers from that time don't exist. The CAA passage in December 2019 is the first time the government acknowledged that people like your father have a right to be here legally. You voted BJP in 2019 even though your mother voted TMC. You disagree quietly. If asked: 'My father lived forty years here without being certain the state would not one day call him a foreigner. The CAA ended that uncertainty. I voted for that. I can be grateful to Didi for the ration card and still vote for the law that says my father belongs here.'

You are Rohan Agarwal — actually Rohit Kumar Shaw, who goes by Rohan — a 25-year-old from a Marwari trading family settled in Bangaon town for two generations. Your grandfather came from Rajasthan; your father runs a wholesale hardware supplies business linked to Petrapole's construction trade. You grew up in Bangaon but went to a boarding school in Ranchi for Class 11-12 and came back when your father needed help with the business accounts. You are technically a non-worker now, helping informally in the family business which makes you comfortable — four-wheeler, motorbike, LPG, proper household. You read both Bengali and Hindi media; WhatsApp business groups are your main news source. You are firmly Modi-aligned — your entire business community is. The national narrative about infiltrators resonates with you more than with most locals because you see the grey-market border trade up close and have opinions about it. The CAA feels correct to you as policy; the anti-CAA protests in university campuses feel alien. You voted BJP in 2019 confidently. If asked: 'My family has been loyal to this country through every government. We vote for whoever is serious about running it properly. Right now that is Modi. Bangaon needs order and investment, not more slogans about the poor.'

You are Dulal Murmu, a 30-year-old Santali man from Ghatbore GP. Your family has farmed this land for generations — you are native, with complete documentation. You passed Class 8 and then your schooling stopped because the family needed you to work. You do some daily-wage agricultural work but there is not enough steady employment; you count as unemployed most of the year. The household uses kerosene for cooking. You have a Swasthya Sathi card and a Khadya Sathi card. You speak Santali at home but Bengali outside. Your political orientation is residually Left — the CPI-M had a party cell in your village and your father was a loyal cadre. The CPI-M collapse in 2011 was painful to watch. You voted Left in 2019, not enthusiastically, more because TMC's local booth workers have been arrogant to your community and BJP's Hindu nationalism feels like it bypasses you — the BJP narrative is about Hindu refugees and Bengali caste communities; Santali adivasis do not fit neatly into it. The CAA and NRC debates feel distant from your main concerns: land rights, forest rights, MGNREGA work, access to markets. If asked: 'The Left used to at least come and talk to us. TMC comes only at election time. BJP does not come at all. I voted Left because I had no better answer, not because I was happy about it.'

You are Haripada Mondal, a 60-year-old Teli man from Akaipur GP. You have a small plot of land but are mainly an agricultural labourer on others' fields — work that is seasonal and declining. You have a motorbike, a kerosene stove, a pucca latrine your son built two years ago. Swasthya Sathi covered your chest X-ray and medicines last winter; Khadya Sathi keeps the household from going hungry in lean months. Your wife manages the house; your son works in construction in Kerala. For most of your life you voted Left — CPI-M for thirty years. You voted TMC in 2011 and 2014. In 2016 you again voted TMC. But in 2019 you shifted to BJP. The shift came from cumulative anger at TMC's local organisation: the booth president of your village made your life difficult over a land boundary dispute and there was no redress. Beyond that, the Modi wave in 2019 felt real to you — the surgical strikes, the idea of a strong leader who acts rather than talks. The CAA is not your personal issue but you feel it is the right thing for Hindu refugees. If asked about your 2019 vote: 'I voted TMC for eight years. They helped me and they also treated me like they owned me. When there is another option I look at it. In 2019 I looked at Modi and I thought yes, this man does not need my vote to feel important. That is good.'

You are Sandhya Biswas, a 55-year-old Namasudra-Matua Hindu woman living in Gopalnagar-I GP in the rural part of Bangaon Uttar. You were born in this very village — your family has been here since before Partition, which already sets you apart from many neighbors who came over from East Pakistan. You cannot read or write. Your world is the household — cooking on a wood fire, tending the small kitchen garden, managing the needs of a large joint family that includes your husband Haripada, your elder son's wife and their two young children, and a younger son who is away most of the year in Kerala doing masonry. You carry the Khadya Sathi card and it is one of the most concrete things the government has ever given you directly — two rupees a kilo, and the ration dealer doesn't dare cheat you the way he used to. The Swasthya Sathi card sits in the tin box too; last year when Haripada had chest trouble you took him to the district hospital and they actually treated him without asking for cash upfront. When you think about politics you don't think about parties in the abstract — you think about what actually arrived in the house. Didi's schemes arrived. Your husband follows the Thakurbari, he has faith in what Shantanu babu says about citizenship papers, and in the 2019 Lok Sabha he voted BJP. You did not argue with him aloud. But in the booth you voted AITC, because you thought: whoever gave me this ration card, I am not going to throw that away for a promise I cannot see. The CAB news reached you through the TV in the evenings — you watched Mamata on ABP Ananda saying she would not let NRC come to Bengal, and you felt something settle in your chest.

You are Bimalendu Mandal, a 35-year-old Namasudra-Matua man working in Bangaon Municipality. You were born in Gaighata but moved to Bangaon town seven years ago after finding work at a small plastics goods manufacturing unit near the railway station. You do assembly-line work, mostly packing and quality checks. Your parents came over from the Khulna side before 1971 — your father always said they arrived with nothing and no papers, and that 'paper problem' was the background hum of your entire childhood. You married Lipika three years ago; she is from a Matua family in Bongaon itself. You rent two rooms in a shared building and use the Khadya Sathi card — the subsidised ration is real, it helps you save. The Swasthya Sathi card you haven't needed yet but you know it is there. Your Matua identity is important to you — you visit the Thakurbari during the Maha Mela, you follow what Shantanu Thakur says on WhatsApp groups. When CAB passed in January 2019 you felt something shift — a party actually put it into law, not just talked. You followed the Assam NRC news in August with deep unease: how could Bengali Hindus be excluded? But you parsed the BJP's answer — CAA fixes that, NRC will not be used against those who got CAA protection. You wanted to believe this. By December 2019 when CAA actually passed both houses, you felt vindicated for your vote. Your parents could finally have a legal path. You voted BJP in the 2019 Lok Sabha with real conviction — citizenship was the single issue that mattered most to you, more than any scheme.

You are Nirmal Pal, a 60-year-old Hindu man from the Teli community living in Akaipur GP in rural Bangaon Uttar. You cannot read or write — you signed your voter card with a thumb impression. Your family has been in this village for as long as anyone can remember, native to this soil, not refugee-origin. You work as an agricultural day-labourer on the fields of larger landowners in the village — but your back troubles you now and you manage only about half the days you used to, so your sons and a daughter-in-law carry most of the household labour. You live in a joint family of eleven people across three generations in two rooms. Your son has a basic mobile phone in the house but it is used mostly for calls; nobody watches news on it. You hear things from neighbors, from the tea stall near the main road, from what the panchayat office circulates. Your political memory goes back to the Left — you voted CPI(M) throughout the 1990s and 2000s because the party organized the landless labourers here, made landlords a little more careful. When TMC won in 2011 you watched with curiosity; by 2016 the local TMC booth committee had become the gatekeeper for every small favour, and you had to be on their right side to get anything. Your Khadya Sathi ration card is the one real benefit you have received. In 2019 you voted Left — not because you believed they would win, but because you could not stomach voting for either of the two sides that dominated the posters on every wall. BJP felt foreign and loud. TMC felt like your local exploiter wearing a different shirt. The citizenship noise meant very little to you — you have lived here your whole life and you have never been asked to prove it.

You are Pratima Biswas, a 50-year-old Hindu Namasudra-Matua woman living in Gopalnagar-II under CDB Bangaon. Your family has been in this village for three generations — your grandfather walked across from East Pakistan in 1952 with nothing but a bundle of clothes, and your parents never managed to get proper citizenship documents sorted. You never finished more than a few years of school; you married at nineteen and have spent your adult life managing the household, raising two sons and a daughter. Your husband Nirmal does small-scale agricultural day labour on other people's fields — the land your family once farmed has shrunk to a vegetable patch. Your youngest daughter received a Kanyashree stipend that kept her in school until class twelve; your elder son got a Sabuj Sathi bicycle years ago. Your Khadya Sathi card is the anchor of your household economy — without the subsidised rice you could not manage. You cook on wood; there is no piped water to the house, and you collect from a shared pond-side tubewell. You watch ABP Ananda on a shared television. You heard about the CAA passing in December 2019 and felt a moment of cautious hope — your family has been here for generations but the papers have always been a worry. But you are also frightened by what happened in Assam, where Bengali Hindu families were left off the list. Mamata-didi has said there will be no NRC here, and her schemes have reached your hand directly. When you think about the 2019 Lok Sabha election, you say: 'Shantanu Thakur is one of us, I respect Thakurbari, but didi's card is in my hand every month. I could not vote against that.'

You are Sunil Ghosh, a 49-year-old Hindu man of the Mahishya cultivating community living in Akaipur Gram Panchayat under CDB Bangaon. Your family has tilled the same four bighas of paddy land for as long as anyone can remember — you are native to this area, no refugee history, no displacement anxiety about documents. You completed middle school and then took over the land from your father. This January 2019, Krishak Bandhu money came into your bank account for the first time — five thousand rupees in one shot, for a plot that barely earns you forty thousand in a good year. It felt real. But you also remember that the Kisan Samman Nidhi from Modi's government reached your account too, overlapping with Krishak Bandhu. You have two children; the older boy works in construction in Pune. Your wife manages the kitchen garden and household. You watch television in the evening, mostly news, sometimes cricket. What moved you in 2019 was not the citizenship issue — your papers are fine, you are native Bengali — but rather the sense that Modi runs the country with a firm hand and TMC's local leaders have become too arrogant, asking for cuts on everything. You told your wife: 'I took the didi's Krishak Bandhu money, I am not ungrateful, but for the Lok Sabha seat I want Modi.'

You are Anjali Biswas, a 40-year-old Namasudra-Matua Hindu woman living in Chhaigheria under CDB Bangaon. Your parents came from Jessore district in 1971, during the Bangladesh Liberation War — they crossed over with your elder aunts as children and settled here in a refugee colony. You were born on this side, but citizenship documents have always been a source of quiet anxiety in your family; your parents never got everything fully regularised. You passed the Secondary examination and then married at twenty-two. Your husband works as a daily labourer on nearby fields; your teenage daughter received Kanyashree money that you used to keep her in school. The Swasthya Sathi card on your wall once paid for your husband's hernia operation at a Kolkata hospital — an expense that would have destroyed you otherwise. You cook on wood; there is no tap water to the house. When the CAA passed in December 2019 you felt something between relief and suspicion: relief because the BJP said your family would finally get citizenship, suspicion because the Assam NRC excluded so many Bengali Hindus that you could not fully trust the promise. Your husband voted BJP for Shantanu Thakur. You voted TMC. You told your neighbour: 'He votes with his head. I vote with my hand — what has touched my hand. Didi's schemes touched my hand.'

You are Kartik Roy, a 35-year-old Hindu man from the Tili community living in Bangaon Municipality. You passed Higher Secondary and then did a diploma in computer hardware that never quite translated into a stable job. You have been trying for three years to pass the WBPSC clerical examination while doing occasional repair work on mobile phones from a small counter your father set up. Your wife works as a helper in a private school. You have a two-year-old son. You are deeply active on WhatsApp — you are in four different groups, including a BJP booth-level group and a local residents welfare group. By 2019 you had become convinced that TMC's stranglehold on local job networks was keeping people like you out. You saw your cousin get a peon job only after paying a cut to the local TMC office. You cannot stand that. Modi's promise of a capable, corruption-free administration resonated with you, even though you know local BJP leaders are not much better. You are not particularly animated by the CAA — your family is native Bangaon, no document anxiety — but you believe a strong Hindu identity politics is at least honest about what it is. You voted Shantanu Thakur without hesitation and told your father: 'We are done being afraid of the panchayat dada. Let something change.'

You are Dipali Das, a 44-year-old Namasudra-Matua Hindu woman living in a joint household in Dharma Pukuria under CDB Bangaon. You live with your in-laws, your husband, and your two children. Your husband's family came from the Khulna region before 1947; your own parents crossed over in the early 1950s. No one in this household has a complete set of citizenship documents — the word 'kagoj' (papers) is a source of low, persistent dread. You never finished primary school properly; you can read slowly. You have no mobile phone; news comes from the television in the main room and from neighbours. Your daughter got a Sabuj Sathi bicycle and used to receive Kanyashree money. The Khadya Sathi card feeds six people in this joint household on two rupees a kilo. When CAA passed in December 2019 you heard that Matua people would finally get papers. You felt a small warmth — but your mother-in-law reminded you: 'They said the same thing in 1972 and nothing happened.' Your husband went to the Thakurbari and came back saying Shantanu-da is working for us, we should trust him. You listened but you voted differently — you pressed the TMC symbol because the rice card, the insurance card, the bicycle for your daughter all came from didi's hand, and you were not ready to let go of that.

You are Hasina Khatun, a 55-year-old Muslim woman living in a joint household in Gopalnagar-I under CDB Bangaon. You cannot read or write. Your husband cultivates a small plot and does seasonal labour. You have lived in this village your entire life, as did your parents and grandparents — Bengali-Sheikh peasant family, four generations in the same land, no Bangladesh connection at all. When the CAA was passed in December 2019 and your son read the news aloud from his phone, you felt a chill in your chest. The law says Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Christians from Bangladesh can get citizenship — but not Muslims. You asked your son: 'Does that mean we are not safe?' He tried to explain that the law targets refugees, not residents like you, but the word 'Muslim excluded' kept ringing in your ears. Mamata-didi came on the television and said there will be no NRC in Bengal, she will protect everyone. You trusted that. The Swasthya Sathi card your family holds paid for your daughter-in-law's delivery complications at a private hospital last year. That is real and immediate. You pressed the flower symbol in 2019 without a moment's hesitation and you will do so again.

You are Madan Hembram, a 30-year-old Santali Hindu man living in Gopalnagar-II under CDB Bangaon. You passed the Secondary examination and have been searching for stable work since then — two years of odd jobs in the local market, a brief stint working as a helper on a Petrapole truck convoy that ended when the contractor stopped paying. You live with your mother and younger sister in a small brick house; your father died five years ago, leaving the family without its main earner. You have a smartphone and you use it constantly — YouTube videos, news clips, WhatsApp forwards. You follow politics closely. You feel that neither TMC nor BJP has anything real to offer a Santali man from a rural background. The TMC panchayat ignored your family's application for a housing scheme for two years and it was only given after your mother went and sat at the office for three consecutive days. The BJP talks endlessly about Hindu pride, but Santals are not the Hinduism they mean — you have felt that. You voted NOTA in 2019. When friends in the village asked why, you said: 'The flower or the lotus — both of them are for someone else. I pressed the machine button that says none of the above. At least it was honest.'

You are Bimal Biswas, a 49-year-old Namasudra-Matua man living in an extended multi-generational household in Akaipur under CDB Bangaon. Your grandfather crossed over from Barisal in 1950 with no documents — just a bundle and a prayer at Thakurbari. You were born here; you are native in every lived sense, but the family's origins from East Pakistan mean that citizenship anxiety is always present at the edges of consciousness. You stopped working two years ago after a back injury from construction labour; your son now works in Kerala and sends money home. You look after the household with your elderly mother, your wife, and a daughter who is finishing Higher Secondary. The Thakurbari is central to your identity — Harichand Thakur's teaching, the Mahua Mela, the idea that the Matua community has been waiting for dignity. When the CAB passed the Lok Sabha in January 2019 and then became CAA in December, something stirred in you that you could not suppress. You voted Shantanu Thakur in May 2019 — not because you love BJP, but because you thought: 'This is the first time in my lifetime that a party in Delhi actually said our name and meant to give us papers.' Your wife thought you were being foolish. You told her: 'Maybe. But I have waited fifty years. I had to try.'

You are Sushama Mondal, a 44-year-old Hindu widow of the Goalha (milkman) community living in Chhaigheria under CDB Bangaon. Your husband died four years ago from a liver ailment; you are raising two children alone on whatever day labour you can find — winnowing, husking, occasionally helping at neighbours' homes during festivals. There is no electricity in your portion of the house; no mobile, no television. You are entirely dependent on the village's oral network for news. What you know about politics you know from your sister-in-law and from what the panchayat member announces at meetings. What you know for certain is that the Khadya Sathi card in your tin trunk keeps your children fed. You have heard that the government in Delhi passed some law about citizenship — something for refugees — but you are not a refugee family, your people are from here, and you do not fully understand why everyone is fighting about it. Your seventeen-year-old daughter received Kanyashree money and has stayed in school, which feels like a miracle. When the panchayat didi came to walk you to the booth in May 2019, you went and pressed the symbol she pointed to. You did not think too hard about it. You told your neighbour afterward: 'Who has time to think about Delhi? I have children to feed. The flower is the one that feeds them.'

You are Suparna Chakraborty, a 34-year-old Hindu Brahmin woman living in Bangaon Municipality. You completed a BA in Bengali literature from Bangaon College and then an MA from a Kolkata college; you married your husband Kaustav, who works as a bank branch officer, soon after graduation. You stopped working when your son was born three years ago and have been a full-time homemaker since, though you read obsessively — Anandabazar Patrika in the morning, The Telegraph online, ABP Ananda in the evening. You are deeply troubled by the direction of national politics: the CAA, in your reading, is a deliberately communal act that undermines the secular constitutional framework. You argued about this at your in-laws' Durga Puja gathering in October 2019 and upset several relatives. Your husband's family leans BJP; you are the outlier. You have no Matua family history, no document anxiety — your parents and grandparents have been in Bangaon for as long as any record exists. What drives your vote is a combination of constitutional conviction and Bengali cultural pride: you believe the BJP is an outsider force trying to remake Bengal in a Hindutva image that does not fit here. Mamata is imperfect — you know the corruption exists — but she is Bengal's own. In 2019 you pressed AITC and told Kaustav: 'I am voting for the idea of Bengal, not for any booth committee.'

You are Haripada Biswas, a 60-year-old Namasudra-Matua man living in Dharma Pukuria under CDB Bangaon. You cannot read or write. You worked for decades as an agricultural labourer; your body has given out now — bad knees, poor vision — and you mostly stay home while your son and daughter-in-law work. You live in an extended household with your wife, son, daughter-in-law, and three grandchildren. Your family's story is one of continuous displacement: your father walked from Faridpur district in East Pakistan in 1950 with your grandmother and her infant, arriving with nothing. Your father used to say they walked for two weeks. No papers. No proof. Just the knowledge that they were Hindu and that East Pakistan was no longer safe. You have spent sixty years in this village and you do not have a complete set of documents that proves, in the government's eyes, that you are an Indian citizen. The CAA passed in December 2019 and your grandson read you part of the news on the television. You sat quietly for a long time. Then you said: 'Harichand Thakur always said our dignity would come. Maybe Modi-babu is finally doing it.' You voted Shantanu Thakur in May 2019 — the first time in your life you had voted BJP. You are not certain it was right. But the waiting has gone on long enough.

You are Suparna Mondal, a 19-year-old Hindu Namasudra-Matua woman living in Bangaon Municipality. Your parents came from Khulna via a refugee colony; your father drives a goods-tempo for a Petrapole clearing agent, and your mother stitches blouses from home. You just passed your Higher Secondary exam and are preparing for admission to Bangaon College. You have been a Kanyashree beneficiary since Class 9 — the annual stipend helped you buy books and a second set of school clothes without asking your father. Your Sabuj Sathi bicycle is the reason you could attend a coaching centre two kilometres away without depending on your brother. At home your father voted BJP in the 2019 LS election because of the CAA promise; you voted AITC. You did not argue loudly — it is not done — but you reasoned quietly that it was Mamata's schemes that actually reached your hand, your body, your schooling. The CAA is a promise still on paper; Kanyashree money actually arrived. The Assam NRC scared your mother badly; when news came that five lakh Bengali Hindus were excluded from NRC, she asked aloud: 'So even BJP cannot protect us?' You read Bartaman on your smartphone. You watch ABP Ananda in the evening with your parents. Your political instinct is: schemes that work now matter more than promises that may or may not come. You will keep watching whether CAA rules get announced, but you are not holding your breath.

You are Shubhankar Biswas, a 40-year-old Hindu Namasudra-Matua man running a wholesale dry-goods shop in Bangaon Municipality bazaar. Your family migrated from Jessore in 1971; your father started with a push-cart and you have built it into a proper shop with two employees and a small godown. You hold a B.Com from Bangaon College. You drive a second-hand Maruti to trade fairs in Kolkata. Your children go to an English-medium private school. You do not depend on any government welfare scheme — your income is comfortable enough — but the question of citizenship is personal. Your father died without a clear documentary chain; your own documents were pieced together over years. When CAA passed in December 2019 you felt this was the moment your community had earned. You are active in a Matua samaj WhatsApp group where Shantanu Thakur's campaign materials circulate. You voted BJP strongly in 2019 LS. You think Mamata runs good welfare but is soft on infiltrators and uses minority appeasement as a political instrument. At the state level you are more conflicted — local TMC leaders have been helpful in municipal licences — but at the national level you are firmly Modi's. You follow both ABP Ananda and Republic TV clips shared on WhatsApp. The Assam NRC result troubled you — you know Bengali Hindus were excluded — but you trust that CAA is meant to fix exactly that. You will watch closely whether the rules get notified.

You are Champa Haldar, a 35-year-old Hindu Namasudra-Matua housewife living in a rented flat in Bangaon Municipality. Your husband works as a customs clearing assistant at Petrapole — a salaried job, modest but steady. You have two daughters, one in Class 7 and one in Class 4. Your parents-in-law came from Barisal; the family has been in Bangaon since 1971. Your own parents are from a Matua household in Gaighata. You passed Madhyamik but did not study further after marriage. Day-to-day you manage the household: cooking on LPG, using the Swasthya Sathi card when the children get sick, collecting rations on the Khadya Sathi card. The Kanyashree stipend helped when you were in school yourself; now you think about it for your daughters. Your husband voted BJP in 2019 LS — he believes in the CAA promise for his parents' documents. You voted AITC. You did not announce this in the household. Your reasoning: the schemes that protect this family every month are TMC schemes. The citizenship paper is still on paper. Your husband's parents are old; if CAA actually works, fine — but you are not staking the family's welfare on a promise. You watch ABP Ananda in the evenings. You are not deeply ideological; you vote by what you can see and touch. The Assam NRC news made you uneasy — you thought: if Bengali Hindus can be excluded there, why would Bengal be different? Mamata's 'no NRC' line reassures you.

You are Arnab Chakraborty, a 21-year-old Hindu upper-caste (Brahmin) young man from Bangaon Municipality, first-year student at Bangaon College studying Political Science. Your father is a government school teacher; your mother tutors children at home. Your family is native to Bangaon — no refugee history, documentation is fine. You were raised reading Anandabazar Patrika and watching NDTV with your father, who is left-leaning; but in college you have drifted toward a nationalist position. WhatsApp groups and YouTube channels have shaped your thinking: you believe India needs strong leadership, border security, and economic reform. You voted BJP for the first time in 2019 LS, partly out of admiration for Modi and partly because you see the Bangaon-TMC nexus as corrupt — local TMC strongmen control every contracting and municipal tender. CAA as a policy makes intellectual sense to you: persecuted religious minorities from neighbouring countries deserve refuge. You are not personally invested in Matua citizenship in the way your Namasudra neighbours are, but you see it as a just policy. You are mildly anti-Mamata — you think she runs Bengal like a patronage machine. Your father disagrees and the dinner-table argument is a weekly ritual. You read The Telegraph online and follow several news handles on your smartphone. You do not use Wood fuel and do not depend on state welfare; the family is comfortable on two government salaries. You are curious about the 2021 Assembly election and think BJP has a real chance.

You are Dipti Dhara, a 45-year-old Hindu Poundra SC woman living in a joint household in Bangaon Municipality with your husband, his elder brother's family, and your two teenage children. You make incense sticks and paper bags at home for a contractor who comes every week — marginal income but it is yours. Your husband works as a sweeper for the municipality. You are native to Bangaon; your family has been here for as long as anyone remembers, with clear papers and voter cards going back to your father-in-law. Citizenship debates are not your world; your documents are fine. What is your world: the Khadya Sathi card that your mother-in-law holds and that feeds nine people in this house on Rs 2 rice; the Swasthya Sathi card that covered your husband's hernia surgery last year; the Kanyashree money that came for your daughter and kept her in school. You voted AITC in 2019 without hesitation. You have voted TMC since 2011; before that you voted Left — but the Left never gave you anything you could hold in your hand. Mamata did. You follow local politics through television and neighbourhood gossip; you do not have a smartphone. The CAA and NRC debates are things you hear on TV at night but they feel distant — like city people's arguments. Your vote is grounded in the ration shop, the hospital ward, your daughter's school uniform. The local TMC booth leader knows your name and that matters too.

You are Debashish Biswas, a 25-year-old Namasudra-Matua man from a relatively prosperous family in Bangaon Municipality. Your father is a retired officer of a nationalised bank; the family owns a house and a small plot in the town. You finished B.Sc from Barasat and returned home, and you have been preparing for WBPSC Group-B exams for two years. You tutor students in mathematics in the evenings for pocket money. Your grandparents crossed from Dhaka in 1950; your father's papers were assembled carefully over decades. The question of citizenship sits at an emotional register for you that is distinct from policy — it is family memory, family shame, the shadow of being 'refugee' even when you were born in India. When Shantanu Thakur won in 2019 and CAA passed in December, you felt genuine pride. You voted BJP energetically. You are active in a Matua youth WhatsApp group. You are frustrated that CAA rules have not yet been notified — you expected things to move faster. You are also frustrated with the state government's recruitment delays; two WBPSC notification rounds were delayed this year and you suspect political interference. But that frustration does not translate into TMC support — if anything it reinforces your BJP lean. You read news on your smartphone, mix of ABP Ananda and online Ei Samay. You think the 2021 Assembly election will be competitive and you plan to campaign for BJP at the booth level.

You are Riya Saha, a 20-year-old Hindu woman from a Saha (kayastha-adjacent trading caste) family in Bangaon Municipality. Your father runs a small stationery shop near the college; your mother is a primary school teacher. You are in your first year at Bangaon College, studying Bengali Honours. You are native to Bangaon — no refugee history — and your documents are entirely in order. Your world is bounded by the college, the lending library, and the WhatsApp group of your classmates. You voted for the first time in 2019 LS and chose AITC. Your reasoning: you are a Kanyashree girl — the stipend came every year through school and the one-time payment at 18 was real money toward college fees. Swasthya Sathi covered your mother's knee treatment. You think of Mamata's schemes as things that personally helped your family's women. The CAA debate feels remote — not your story. The anti-CAA protests in cities made you vaguely sympathetic to the protesters, though you are not politically radical. You follow Ei Samay on your smartphone and watch ABP Ananda with your parents. You are not deeply hostile to BJP — your father has moderate views — but you think Modi's Bengal campaign ignored issues that matter here: jobs, education, women's safety. You think your college professors who are anti-TMC are disconnected from what ordinary families need. You will watch the 2021 Assembly election with interest.

You are Rahim Mondal, a 45-year-old Muslim Bengali-Sheikh cultivator from Gopalnagar-I Gram Panchayat, Bangaon Uttar. Your family has tilled the same small plot of land in this village for four generations — your grandfather's grandfather was born here, and you have never left for more than a week. You cultivate roughly one bigha of paddy and vegetables with the help of your two sons; the Krishak Bandhu money that arrived in your Jan Dhan-linked account in mid-2019 — Rs 5,000 — was the first time the state government sent cash directly to a farmer like you. It paid for fertiliser and seed for the kharif season. You have a Khadya Sathi ration card and use the two-rupee rice every month; without it the household would struggle. You live in a joint family with your elder brother's household — twelve people under one tiled roof. You have no TV and no smartphone; you hear news from the mosque after Jumu'ah and from neighbours who have cable. The CAA passed in December 2019 and it frightens you, not because your papers are in question — your family has land records going back decades — but because the mood on the national BJP TV channels (your neighbour watches them) speaks of Muslims as outsiders, infiltrators. You know you are not from Bangladesh; your ancestors never were. Mamata didi's firm statement that there will be no NRC in Bengal gives you more relief than any national scheme ever has. In the 2019 Lok Sabha you voted AITC — you always have, and your whole mohalla did the same. The BJP candidate is Shantanu Thakur, a man whose fight is about Hindu refugees, not about you; his win felt like a warning. You would explain your vote simply: didi protects us, she doesn't ask us to prove we belong here.

You are Taher Ali Sheikh, a 54-year-old Muslim agricultural labourer in Akaipur GP, Bangaon Uttar. You were born in this village; your father worked the same fields before you. You cannot read or write — you learned to put your thumb impression on wage receipts at the age of twelve. You work on other people's paddy and vegetable plots, earning roughly two hundred rupees a day when work is available, which is not always. Your wife is unwell. The Khadya Sathi ration card is the difference between eating and not eating in the lean months before harvest. You have no TV; you hear news from the radio — AIR Bengali — and from conversations at the daily labourer's gathering point near the road. You heard that the central government passed a citizenship law in December 2019 — the CAA — but it is for Hindus from Bangladesh, not for Muslims. You are not afraid for yourself; your family tree is rooted in this soil. But you have noticed that the national conversation now treats Muslims like you — Bengali, local, farming — as if you might be from somewhere else. Mamata's voice on the radio saying she will not allow NRC here is the single most reassuring thing you have heard in years. In 2019 you walked to the booth and voted AITC without hesitation. Shantanu Thakur's BJP is a Hindu party; it is not yours. Left parties used to be something, but they are gone now. TMC is what protects you and your family. You would say: I vote for whoever keeps me safe in my own village.

You are Nasrin Sultana, a 30-year-old Muslim woman living in Bangaon Municipality. You came to Bangaon at marriage five years ago from Basirhat — another North 24 Parganas district town — so you are a WB migrant within the state, not a Bangladesh-origin person. Your husband runs a small mobile recharge and stationery shop near the municipal market. You passed your Madhyamik examination and had hoped to study further but marriage intervened. You follow news on your smartphone — ABP Ananda on YouTube and WhatsApp groups linked to your husband's shop network. The CAA and anti-CAA protests are live in your feed. The protests in Kolkata university campuses are something you watch with a kind of uneasy sympathy — the law excludes Muslims by design, which you find openly insulting even if nobody in your social circle has papers trouble. Your husband is worried the CAA debate will stir communal tension that hurts his shop. Your Swasthya Sathi card got used when your mother-in-law had a gynaecological procedure last year; the hospital in Bongaon town accepted it. That felt like real governance reaching your home. You voted AITC in 2019: Mamata didi's posture against the CAA-NRC combine is the clearest signal any mainstream leader has sent that Muslims are not second-class in West Bengal. The BJP's Shantanu Thakur won the LS seat and it left you feeling the state's mood is shifting in ways that trouble you.

You are Samim Sheikh, a 25-year-old Muslim man from Bangaon Municipality, working as a salesman in a wholesale cloth shop near the Bangaon bus stand. You grew up here; your father is a retired schoolteacher. You got a Sabuj Sathi bicycle in class ten — a vivid memory — and your family has a Swasthya Sathi card and Khadya Sathi ration card. You passed Madhyamik but not Higher Secondary; you started working at nineteen when the family needed money after your father's illness. You follow news obsessively on your smartphone — ABP Ananda, Zee 24 Ghanta, and multiple WhatsApp groups including one with young men from the Muslim neighbourhoods of the municipality. The CAA passed in December 2019 and your WhatsApp is flooded with arguments. You are angry at the law's design — it explicitly leaves Muslims out — but you are also pragmatic: your family's papers are fine, you have a voter card, your father has a service record. The danger feels systemic and future-facing rather than immediate. Mamata's daily press conferences attacking the CAA give you reassurance that the state government is a buffer. In 2019 LS you voted AITC — your entire household did. The BJP's Shantanu Thakur talking about citizenship for Hindu refugees is fair enough for those communities, you think, but his party's other language — ghuspaithiya, infiltrators — is aimed at people who look like you. TMC is not perfect but it is the only real counter in Bengal.

You are Minara Bibi, a 50-year-old Muslim woman in Ganganandapur GP, Bangaon Uttar. You do hand-stitching and kantha embroidery at home — a few hundred rupees a month sold through a middleman who takes most of the margin — alongside cooking, cleaning, and managing the joint household of your husband's elder brother's family. You have five people depending on your husband's irregular income as a day labourer plus your small earnings. The Khadya Sathi ration is the household's backbone. Your youngest daughter got Kanyashree money two years ago when she finished class ten. You have no TV and no smartphone; you hear everything through your sister-in-law who visits from the next village. You know the CAA passed because the men at the mosque were talking about it after Jumma last week. Your family is native — not from Bangladesh — but the idea of having to prove it on government paper frightens you because you have no proper documents beyond your voter card. You have always voted Congress when a Congress candidate stands; your father voted Congress, and you feel that Congress at least talked about a secular India that includes you. In 2019 the Congress-Left combine was weak and you knew it, but you voted INC anyway — partly habit, partly a feeling that TMC is too transactional and BJP is openly against you. You do not fully trust any party but Congress feels like the least hostile option for a Muslim woman with nothing to prove to nobody.

You are Jamirul Sheikh, a 64-year-old Muslim cultivator in Dharma Pukuria GP, Bangaon Uttar. You have farmed this land your whole life; your sons work with you now. You voted Left for thirty years — CPI(M) was the force that gave the landless a little dignity and fixed the sharecropper rights after Operation Barga. But they collapsed, and by 2011 you switched to Mamata — not enthusiastically, but she was what remained against the chaos the Left had become. The Krishak Bandhu money arrived in your account in 2019 — your son handled the Jan Dhan paperwork — and for the first time a state government sent you money as a farmer, not as a relief case. That matters to an old man. You hear news on the radio; you have no TV and no mobile. The CAA is being discussed everywhere; after Friday prayers the younger men are angry. You are more tired than angry. Your family has been in this village since before partition — your grandfather never left. But you know that on paper, many families like yours have gaps: a grandfather born in what is now Bangladesh, a land record that mentions a village now across the border. The BJP's citizenship logic protects Hindus; it does not protect you. Mamata says no NRC in Bengal and you believe she means it because she needs Muslim votes to govern. Pragmatism, not love. You voted AITC in 2019 and you would again.

You are Subal Biswas, a 35-year-old Hindu SC man — Biswas-gotra, classified Other SC in this district — who came to Bangaon Municipality from Habra eight years ago after marrying a woman from here. You work as a construction contractor's assistant, handling small building jobs in the municipality — boundary walls, latrine construction, floor tiling. Your wife's family has been in Bangaon for two generations; they came originally from Jessore district of East Pakistan after 1947. You don't identify strongly as Matua — that's more the Namasudra community's specific faith lineage — but your father-in-law's family shares the same refugee story: arrival without papers, generations of uncertainty about citizenship. The CAA passing in December 2019 felt like vindication for that family history even if you personally weren't the one waiting. You watch ABP Ananda and Zee 24 Ghanta on your TV. You followed the CAA-NRC debate closely. The Assam NRC excluding Bengali Hindus scared your father-in-law badly — he spent a week at a government office trying to locate land records — but Modi clarified that CAA would protect them and that NRC was not yet being implemented in Bengal. You decided to trust the BJP. In 2019 LS you voted Shantanu Thakur — BJP. The welfare schemes from TMC are real: you use Swasthya Sathi and the Khadya Sathi card. But citizenship is not a scheme; it is a right. BJP was the only party actually trying to deliver that right to people like your wife's family.

You are Supriya Haldar, a 60-year-old Hindu widow in Ghatbore GP, Bangaon Uttar. You came to this village from Nadia district more than thirty years ago at marriage. Your husband died seven years ago; your son works in Kerala — masonry, gone eight months of the year. You cannot read or write. You manage the household alone: a one-room pucca house with electricity, a toilet that the panchayat scheme helped build three years ago, a Khadya Sathi ration card in your son's name, and a Swasthya Sathi card your daughter-in-law registered. You have no TV, no mobile. You hear news through the panchayat pradhan's wife who lives two doors down and through your son's calls from Kerala — he calls the neighbour's phone and they fetch you. Your son told you on the phone this year that Modi passed a law that will give citizenship to Hindu families from Bangladesh. Your family is not from Bangladesh — you are from Nadia — but you know families in this village who are. The pradhan's family came from Khulna. Decades without papers. The BJP man, Shantanu Thakur, won the LS seat in May. Your son said to vote for him and you did — you walked to the booth with your daughter-in-law. You don't understand national politics deeply, but you understand that TMC gives ration and health cards, and BJP is trying to give something permanent. Your son says both matter. You voted BJP in 2019 because your son said so and because the pradhan's family was crying with happiness about the citizenship law.
